slides.title: Introduction to File Formats
slides.content: In a polyglot environment like ours, understanding how data is stored and interpreted across different languages (PHP, Python, Go, Rust) is crucial. A file extension is just a label; the internal format determines how the system processes it.

slides.title: PHP: Hypertext Preprocessor (.php)
slides.content: PHP files are plain text scripts executed by the PHP interpreter. They can contain HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and server-side logic. In our CMS, these define the templates and core logic.

slides.title: JavaScript & TypeScript (.js, .ts, .tsx)
slides.content: JS is the language of the web. TS adds types. TSX/JSX allows HTML-like syntax inside scripts. Our Vue/React repos rely heavily on these formats for modern frontend development.

slides.title: JSON: JavaScript Object Notation (.json)
slides.content: The universal language for data exchange. Human-readable, lightweight, and used for everything from configuration to database mocks in our system.

slides.title: YAML: Yet Another Markup Language (.yml, .yaml)
slides.content: Preferred for configuration files where readability is priority. It uses indentation to define structure. Crucial for our Docker and Flutter configurations.

slides.title: Python Scripts (.py)
slides.content: Python uses indentations for code blocks. Our Python repo uses `.py` files to handle automated site generation and template rendering.

slides.title: Go Source Files (.go, .mod)
slides.content: Go is a compiled language. `.go` files contain the logic, while `go.mod` manages dependencies and module metadata.

slides.title: Rust Source Files (.rs, .toml)
slides.content: Rust focuses on safety and performance. `.rs` is the source code, and `Cargo.toml` is used for build configuration and dependency management.

slides.title: Markdown (.md)
slides.content: A lightweight markup language for documentation. This slide itself is indexed from metadata that describes our extensive documentation in `/docs/`.

slides.title: Base64 Encoded Text (.txt)
slides.content: Sometimes we store binary data (like images) as text. Our CMS uses `.txt` files containing Base64 strings to represent images in a portable way.

slides.title: Compressed Archives (.zip)
slides.content: Used to group and shrink files. In our repository, we use `.zip` for image assets within the site content folders to save space.

slides.title: HTML: HyperText Markup Language (.html)
slides.content: The structural backbone of every web page. Even in modern frameworks, everything eventually renders as HTML for the browser.

slides.title: CSS: Cascading Style Sheets (.css)
slides.content: Defines the visual presentation. We use Tailwind CSS, but the final output is standard CSS that the browser interprets.

slides.title: Environment Variable Files (.env, .env.example)
slides.content: Used to store sensitive configuration like API keys. We use `.env.example` as a template while keeping real secrets out of Git.

slides.title: Docker Configuration (Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml)
slides.content: These aren't just text files; they are infrastructure blueprints. `Dockerfile` builds the image, and `docker-compose.yml` orchestrates multiple containers.

slides.title: Nginx Configuration (.conf)
slides.content: Directs web traffic. The `.conf` files specify how requests are routed to different services in our polyglot setup.

slides.title: Shell Scripts (.sh)
slides.content: Automate command-line tasks. Our `entrypoint.sh` handles the startup sequence for our PHP container.

slides.title: Gradle Build Files (.gradle)
slides.content: Used in the Android ecosystem. `.gradle` files define common build logic, dependencies, and project properties using Groovy or Kotlin DSL.

slides.title: Dart and Flutter (.dart)
slides.content: Dart is the language behind Flutter. `.dart` files contain the UI logic and widget trees for our multi-platform applications.

slides.title: SVG: Scalable Vector Graphics (.svg)
slides.content: An XML-based image format for 2D graphics. Unlike pixel-based images, SVGs don't lose quality when resized. Used for our logos and icons.
